title: Aptera Stock Implodes On Stock Offering $SEV

transcript.text: All right. Hello and welcome back to the

going to be the startup aera.
So, we made a podcast on Atera about

background, APA is a company that has
been around for a while and their
business model is that they're trying to
design and build a very small tricycle
that is powered by electric that is
powered by solar panels. So basically
the idea is they're trying to design
this tricycle that has two seats to be
extremely lightweight, very uh very
small and very efficient to the point
where the amount of energy that it
generates from its solar panels that are
mounted on the roof and the hood and
things of that nature will be enough so
that you never have to spend money on
fuel or electricity to recharge the
battery. Um and you can just drive it
basically for free. So that's the idea.
However, uh as we explained at length in
our previous podcast, there are very
very strong reasons to doubt the
company's claimed efficiency levels and
it's just just um almost inconceivable
that some of the uh some of the claims
that they've made about how how much you
can drive on it on solar energy um are
true. And to date, they have not yet
they have not released a production
ready product.
As far as the company's operations and
uh financial situation, the company has
been a dumpster fire since they
basically since inception, they haven't
really been able to raise any money from
uh legitimate institutional investors.
And so to fund their cash burn, they
have historically funded all their
operations through equity crowdfunding.
So that means basically selling their
stock to individual investors,
accredited or nonacredited investors.
basically selling stock to their fans,
people who like the idea of the product
and therefore are more willing to hand
over money to invest in the company's
equity. Um, however, over the course of
many years, they still have not been
able to come out with an actual product
and they've basically run out of people
willing to u give over their money
through these equity crowdfunding
campaigns and that source of funding has
dried up. And so just a few months ago
on October 16th, they finally went
public via a direct listing which uh on
the one hand gives the potential to
raise money from the public public
public equity markets in the future.
However, it also gave their existing
shareholders finally a way to sell all
these shares that they they'd
accumulated from the crowdfunding
campaigns. So, as expected in the months
following the direct listing, the the uh
Atera stock price uh fell uh over the

the past couple weeks, they announced a
$9 million public offering of their
stock. And on that announcement, the
share price collapsed about 70%. So ve a
very very bad situation even when
they're trying to raise a relatively
small amount of money $9 million. Keep
in mind that as we as we explained in
the pre our previous podcast they need
about $65 million according to
themselves to get a full a fullscale
production ready uh production line
going. And if they have this much
trouble just to raise $9 million, a

causes their share price to tank 70%.
Then that just that just shows they're
in a very very tough situation in terms
of being, you know, the faint glimmer of

terra. and to um talk about a little bit

>> All right. Thank you. And now that Ryan

Well, they've made today almost no

that's not enough, you know, to get them

if you notice there's, you know, workers
are,

Now, let's go into the weeds of, you

8 million new shares uh which they could
which they expected to raise $17

And you know during this time the deal,

we're gonna raise $17 million, but that

dollars." Then the stock price by

And on the day that they released this
press release on January 23rd, right
after they released it, the stock opened
at like a little over $2. the stock
tanked again down another 40% and then
it just in that one day and then it
closed the day at $1.50 because people
could see oh there's this massive amount

dilution is bad for existing
shareholders. So then people started
selling shares and causing the price to

difficult it is when you have a company
like Optera. like, "Oh, okay. We go we
list our shares on the NASDAQ. That

we're going to fund our, you know, $65
million investment plan to get up to low

time you announce an offering, that
causes more dilution, which causes the
share price to decline because people
can can see the dilution is incoming.
And then as the share price declines,
then you have to issue even more shares
to raise the same amount of capital,
which caused the share price to decline
even more. It's basically this death
spiral. Now
this $9 million remember they need $6.5
million or they're sorry they need $65

and this is only $9 million. They still

given how much the share price has

because as they do more stock offerings
over the coming months, the share price
is going to go lower and lower and
lower. So this is Aera has entered the

hyper diluting and your stock price

don't think I personally don't think

uh 2006, I think. And then it went
